They reflect on how these innovations connect to place, identity, and value.

Students create short, dynamic video stories in Adobe Express, weaving together poetry, images, and narration to express their personal or cultural connection to a chosen place. They can highlight how perspectives on land and place differ across Australia and its neighbours.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'moving map' or interactive diorama with Lego Spike Prime. The model physically demonstrates how boundaries, landmarks, or cultural sites can be represented and valued differently (e.g., programmable markers that light up or move to show First Nations Countries vs. state borders). Students document their process and learning in a digital journal.

plannerText: This unit explores how land and place are represented and valued in different ways, focusing on Australia's states, territories, First Nations Countries, and neighbouring regions. Students investigate cultural, historical, and geographical perspectives through mapping, poetry, research, and creative digital projects, with a strong emphasis on First Nations connections to Country and diverse ways of understanding place.

ci: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.
lo: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on health; Actions to maintain a healthy mind and body

s.d: Students create an interactive 'Choose Your Own Adventure' scenario about daily health choices using Delightex, then use Canva to visually enhance each decision point with infographics and illustrations. This empowers students to explore the real-life consequences of their choices on health and wellbeing in a dynamic, gamified way.

Students document their personal health journeys by creating digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, voice recordings, and reflections on their food, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness routines. This allows for multimodal self-assessment and sharing with families via Seesaw.

Learners use Micro:bit to track and reflect on their health and wellbeing choices, programming devices to log steps, sleep, or mindfulness activities. The 'Being Active' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/being-active/) empowers students to set personal goals and visualise progress, making healthy habits tangible and motivating.

Students design and distribute a wellbeing survey to classmates and families, collecting data on sleep, nutrition, and activity habits. They then analyse the results in class, using the findings to spark discussions about trends, challenges, and opportunities for healthier choices.

Students create vibrant digital posters or short video PSAs in Adobe Express, advocating for a specific healthy habit (e.g., 'Drink More Water!' or 'Screen-Free Bedtime'). These can be shared on Seesaw or displayed around the school to inspire action.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and code a 'Wellbeing Maze' with Sphero BOLT robots. Each section of the maze represents a health choice (e.g., nutritious snack vs. junk food, active play vs. screen time), and students must program the Sphero to navigate the healthiest path, reflecting on the impact of each decision.

plannerText: This unit explores how the choices and actions students make contribute to their health and wellbeing. Activities include investigating nutrition, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness, engaging with guest speakers, analysing data, and creating personal health action plans. Students reflect on their learning and take action to improve their own wellbeing.

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

s.d: Launch a collaborative 'Virtual Art Gallery of the Imagination' using Delightex for students to design immersive, interactive 3D art installations inspired by their poetry, music, or visual art. Students then embed their creations into a Padlet, where peers can 'walk through' and leave reflective comments, fostering a digital curation experience that mirrors a real-world art show.

Students create digital artist statements and visual reflections using Canva, combining images of their own artwork, poetry, and excursion photos with layered text and design elements to communicate the mood, message, and inspiration behind their creative pieces.

App smash Book Creator with the iPad camera to produce multimedia poetry anthologies. Students record themselves reading their poems, add photos from the NGV excursion, and annotate artworks with their interpretations, creating a rich, interactive eBook for sharing at the art show.

Facilitate a 'Lyric & Art Mashup' project where students use Adobe Express to design animated lyric videos. They select a song (e.g., from the Bruno Mars lyric analysis), illustrate key lines with digital drawings, and animate transitions to visually express the song's mood and meaning.

Host a 'Response Wall' on Seesaw where students upload short video reflections after each art experience (excursion, incursion, or class activity), responding to prompts about how the artwork made them think or feel, and giving peer feedback to deepen collective understanding.

STEM Design Cycle Challenge: Students use Lego Spike Prime to prototype an interactive kinetic sculpture that responds to sound or movement. They follow the Design Cycle to ideate, build, test, and refine a sculpture that visually reacts to music or audience interaction, connecting artistic expression with engineering and technology.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in diverse artistic expressions, including poetry, visual art inspired by Yayoi Kusama, music composition, and excursions to the NGV and State Theatre. Students analyse, create, and respond to art forms, culminating in a student-led art show where they display and reflect on their own creative works.

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of community spaces (like a council chamber or emergency services hub) using Merge Cubes, then create an augmented reality 'choose your own adventure' story in Book Creator where users make decisions as community leaders, exploring the impact of different rules and services on fairness and safety.

Students collaboratively design and distribute a digital survey to gather data from peers and families about which community rules and services are most valued, then analyse the results to inform a class debate on effective community organisation.

Create a digital 'Community Noticeboard' where students post and respond to real-world scenarios involving rules, laws, and services, encouraging peer-to-peer discussion and reflection on fairness and safety in communities.

Students work in teams to design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new community service or law, using Canva's design tools and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling arguments.

Students explore the stories of Nobel Peace Laureates and build their own virtual community in Minecraft Education, making decisions about rules, laws, and services to ensure fairness and safety for all citizens.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype an automated community service robot (e.g., a recycling sorter or safety patrol bot) with Lego Spike Prime, iterating on their design to address a real-world community need and presenting their process and solution to the class.

plannerText: This unit explores how communities create rules, laws, and services to help people live together fairly and safely. Students investigate effective leadership, strengths, and actions, engage in scenarios and debates about community organisation, and take action through persuasive speeches and collaborative projects.

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of their animal enrichment prototypes using Merge Cubes, then document the design process, material choices, and environmental impact in a digital science journal on Book Creator. This app smash allows students to virtually 'hold' and explore their creations while reflecting on sustainable design choices.

s.t: Minecraft: Its Good To Be Green
s.d: Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, analyzing how material choices and design decisions impact the environment. They can then reflect on how these principles could be applied to their own enrichment toy prototypes.

Students create visually engaging infographics or posters in Canva to communicate the life cycle of materials used in their prototypes, highlighting which are biodegradable, recyclable, or reusable, and the impact these choices have on the natural world.

Students program Sphero BOLT robots to simulate animal movement and interaction with different enrichment toy prototypes. This hands-on coding activity helps them test and iterate their designs for functionality and animal engagement.

Students design and test sustainable product prototypes using Micro:bit sensors to measure environmental impact or product performance. The 'Energy Awareness' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/energy-awareness/) guides learners to monitor energy use and consider eco-friendly design, connecting STEM innovation to real-world sustainability challenges.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype an animal enrichment toy or feeding device with sustainable materials, iterating their design based on research and testing. They create a digital 3D model (using Tinkercad or similar) and print their final design, then evaluate its impact on animal wellbeing and the environment.

plannerText: Students investigate how material properties influence product design, research sustainable solutions, and document their learning in digital science journals. They experiment with different materials, compare animal enrichment toys, and ultimately design and prototype their own enrichment device for animals, considering both functionality and environmental impact.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: Diversity of First Nations people; Importance of community, family and traditions; Importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students create interactive Dreamtime stories or virtual dioramas using Merge Cubes, overlaying 3D symbols, animals, and landscapes significant to First Nations cultures. They then app smash by documenting their creative process and cultural reflections in Book Creator, combining AR visuals, text, and audio explanations to deepen understanding of Country, language, and community.

Design a collaborative digital mural in Canva where each student contributes a panel representing an aspect of First Nations identity, such as language, Country, or community. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to incorporate student photos or artwork, and layer in authentic Indigenous symbols and patterns for a visually rich, respectful mural.

Build a living digital map on Padlet where students pin locations of different First Nations groups, add audio snippets of local languages, and share research on traditions, family structures, and connections to Country. Encourage students to comment and ask questions to foster inquiry and respectful dialogue.

Students record video reflections or interviews with family members about their own connections to place, language, and community, then compare and contrast with First Nations perspectives. Use Seesaw to curate a class gallery of these stories, highlighting diversity and shared values.

Students explore how First Nations peoples' knowledge of Country supports biodiversity by building 'beetopias' in Minecraft Education. They apply Indigenous perspectives on land stewardship and sustainable habitats, then reflect on the importance of community and tradition in environmental care.

Students use Micro:bit to create interactive digital stories or dioramas that bring First Nations connections to Country, language, and community to life. The 'Nature Art' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/nature-art/) inspires learners to blend coding, art, and storytelling, deepening their appreciation for Indigenous perspectives through creative technology.

plannerText: Students explore the diversity, traditions, and connections of First Nations people to Country, language, and community through Dreamtime stories, art, excursions, and hands-on experiences. They create narratives, dioramas, and digital projects to synthesise their learning about Indigenous perspectives and cultural significance.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to discoveries, opportunities and new understandings.
lo: Reasons for exploration; How people have explored over time; Consequences of exploration

s.d: Students create interactive 3D exploration artefacts using Merge Cubes, embedding digital content (audio, video, text) about famous explorers or key discoveries. They then app smash by documenting their process and reflections in a multimedia Book Creator journal, allowing peers to 'hold' and explore their artefacts virtually.

Students explore the Minecraft Hall of Inventions world, investigating key innovations from history. They then design and build their own 'invention' inspired by a historical explorer or era, reflecting on how exploration led to new opportunities and understandings. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions

Students design visually engaging digital timelines of major explorations, using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' feature to create layered, annotated visuals that highlight the impact and consequences of exploration across time.

Classes collaborate on a live Padlet 'Exploration Wall', where students post research, questions, and multimedia findings about different explorers, mapping connections between historical events, motivations, and outcomes in real time.

Learners use Micro:bit to map explorers' journeys and simulate the challenges of exploration, coding devices to represent discoveries and obstacles along the way. The 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/data-handling/) enables students to visualise and analyse exploration data, connecting historical inquiry with digital innovation.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and build a robotic model of an exploration tool (e.g., a compass, rover, or ship) with Lego Spike Prime. They iterate on their designs to solve a specific exploration challenge, documenting each stage in their digital journals.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in the concept of exploration, focusing on why people explore, how exploration has changed over time, and the consequences of these journeys. Students investigate historical and modern explorers, engage in mapping and narrative writing, and present their findings through a variety of digital and creative formats.

ci: People's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life.
lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

s.d: Students create interactive 3D Merge Cube experiences representing different places of worship or symbols from world religions. They then app smash by embedding videos, audio reflections, and AR screenshots into a digital Book Creator 'Belief Museum', allowing students to virtually 'hold' and explore diverse beliefs in their hands.

Launch a global 'Belief Wall' where students post digital postcards about their own beliefs, family traditions, or research on world religions. Encourage multimedia posts (audio, video, images) and invite respectful questions and comments to foster intercultural dialogue and empathy.

Students design visually stunning infographics comparing the core beliefs, symbols, and practices of major world religions. Use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create digital collages of places of worship, and present findings in a class 'Belief Gallery Walk'.

Curate a playlist of short documentaries and virtual tours of places of worship from Clickview. Students use the platform's interactive video features to annotate, ask questions, and reflect on similarities and differences between spiritual practices.

Students record video interviews with family members or community leaders about their beliefs and traditions using the iPad camera. They upload and annotate these interviews in Seesaw, tagging key vocabulary and reflecting on how beliefs shape identity.

Design Cycle STEM Challenge: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Celebration Machine' with Lego Spike Prime, programmed to perform a unique movement or display for a chosen religious or cultural festival. They document their process and iterations in a digital journal, reflecting on how technology can celebrate and respect diversity.

plannerText: This unit explores how people's beliefs contribute to the diversity of human life, focusing on spiritual and religious beliefs, similarities and differences among religions, and the impact of these beliefs on human diversity. Students engage in gallery walks, virtual tours, research, and creative projects to deepen understanding and foster respect for different worldviews.

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

s.d: Students design and prototype interactive 3D models of disaster-resistant shelters or emergency tools using Tinkercad, then bring their creations to life with Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record AR video walkthroughs using the iPad camera, narrating their design thinking and how their solution meets a specific community need. This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.

Students create persuasive digital pitch decks for their UNICEF-inspired design solutions, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to showcase their prototypes and integrate infographics, maps, and empathy maps. This supports the pitch-writing and presentation phase, allowing students to visually communicate their process and impact.

Throughout the inquiry, students collaboratively document their ideation, research, and empathy mapping on a shared Padlet board. They can upload photos of their prototypes, maps, and annotated infographics, creating a living portfolio that tracks their design journey and peer feedback.

Teams publish interactive digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to document each stage of the design thinking process. This supports reflection, self-assessment, and sharing with the wider school community.

Students prototype and test their natural hazard solutions using Micro:bit sensors to collect real-time data on environmental conditions. The 'Energy Awareness' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/energy-awareness/) empowers learners to monitor and optimise their designs, making the design thinking process more authentic and evidence-based.

For the STEM Design Cycle project, students use Lego Spike Prime to engineer and prototype a disaster-resilient structure or emergency response robot. They apply the full design thinking cycle: empathising with stakeholders, ideating, prototyping, testing for stability or function (e.g., earthquake shake table or flood simulation), and refining based on feedback. This hands-on challenge directly links to the unit's focus on purposeful design for community needs.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how people design for purpose through the lens of natural hazards and community needs. They engage in the design thinking process, research real-world problems, collaborate in teams, prototype innovative solutions, and pitch their ideas to an authentic audience, integrating empathy, data analysis, and persuasive communication throughout.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students design and build interactive 3D models of their 'ideal community' or economic system using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They then use the iPad camera to record a virtual guided tour, narrating how wealth, resources, and responsibilities are distributed in their imagined society. This merges tactile, spatial, and digital storytelling for a futuristic approach to economic systems.

Launch a collaborative 'Wealth is More Than Money' digital wall where students post multimedia reflections (audio, drawings, photos of their Pop Art, or short videos) exploring non-monetary forms of wealth in their lives and communities. Students then use Seesaw to create digital portfolios tracking their evolving understanding of economic systems and personal values.

Students produce an 'Economic Voices' podcast, simulating interviews with historical and contemporary figures about economic systems, wealth, and responsibilities. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that bring economic concepts to life through storytelling and debate.

Students explore the evolution of economic systems by visiting the virtual museum in Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions. They research a key economic innovation (e.g., currency, banking, trade networks), then build their own invention in the museum, explaining its impact on society and connecting it to their classroom inquiry. This fosters historical perspective and creative application.

Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Smart Marketplace' using Lego Spike Prime. They design and code automated systems (e.g., a robotic cashier, inventory tracker, or sustainable delivery vehicle) that address real-world economic challenges such as fair trade, accessibility, or environmental impact. Teams document their process and pitch their solution to the class, integrating technology, economics, and design thinking.

plannerText: Students inquire into economic systems by exploring wealth beyond money, responsibilities in managing income, and diverse ways income is earned and managed. They engage in provocations, debates, budgeting simulations, job applications, and collaborative projects to design ideal communities, culminating in creative presentations and hands-on design challenges that connect economic concepts to real-world contexts.

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of natural disasters (e.g., bushfires, earthquakes, floods) using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app, then record AR video explanations using the iPad camera. They can app smash with Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to place themselves inside their AR scenes, narrating the science and human impact for a truly immersive digital artifact.

Students use Minecraft Education's 'Invisible Forces At Work' lesson to explore magnetism and gravity, then design a magnetic structure that could protect the International Space Station or assist astronauts. This directly connects to the unit's focus on the interactions between materials and forces in the real world.

Students document their design thinking journey by creating multimedia digital books that combine photos of prototypes, data graphs, scientific explanations, and personal reflections on how their solutions address real-world problems caused by natural disasters.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Wonder Wall' on Padlet, posting questions, research findings, and video clips from Clickview about natural hazards, then use Padlet's mapping feature to visually track disaster locations and impacts across Australia.

Students use Micro:bit to simulate and monitor the effects of natural disasters, collecting and analysing data to inform their disaster response solutions. The 'Volcano Animations' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/volcano-animations/) brings the science of bushfires, earthquakes, and floods to life, making risk analysis and STEM problem-solving hands-on and engaging.

Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype and test earthquake-resistant buildings with Lego Spike Prime. They iterate on their designs based on data from simulated 'shake table' tests, documenting their process and results in a digital engineering journal.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the science of natural disasters, focusing on bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. Through collaborative inquiry, students research hazards, analyse data, map risks, and use the design thinking process to prototype and pitch solutions that protect communities and uphold children's rights. The unit integrates maths, science, geography, and digital technologies, culminating in student-led presentations and reflections.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local flora and fauna using the Merge Cube and iPads, then use the Merge Explorer app to overlay digital information about conservation needs and stewardship actions. They can record video tours of their models using the iPad camera, narrating how their community can interact with and protect these species.

Students collaboratively research and document local council sustainability initiatives on Padlet, then design persuasive digital posters in Canva to promote their chosen initiative to the school community, integrating real data and student-generated visuals.

After a nature walk around the school, students use Book Creator to compile a multimedia field guide of native plants and animals, including photos, audio reflections, and conservation tips tailored to their local environment.

Students explore how bees interact with their environment by applying their knowledge of stewardship and conservation to design and build digital 'beetopias' in Minecraft, supporting thriving bee colonies and reflecting on the importance of biodiversity.

Learners use Micro:bit to monitor local environmental data as they investigate community interactions with the environment, collecting information on temperature, light, or soil moisture. The 'Healthy Oceans' lesson (https://microbit.org/lessons/healthy-oceans/) inspires students to design digital solutions for conservation and stewardship, connecting action projects to real-world data and advocacy.

s.t: Lego Spike Prime Design Cycle: Eco-Innovation Challenge
s.d: Using the Design Cycle, students identify a local environmental issue (e.g., waste, water use, habitat loss), brainstorm solutions, and prototype an eco-innovation using Lego Spike Prime. They test, refine, and present their models, justifying design choices based on community values and conservation needs.

plannerText: Students inquire into how communities interact with the environment, focusing on conservation, stewardship, and the influence of community values on environmental management. Key activities include exploring First Nations perspectives, researching local flora and fauna, investigating council initiatives, and pitching persuasive sustainability actions for the school community.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students create interactive 3D artefacts representing key resources discovered in Australia (e.g., gold, wheat, wool) using Merge Cube. They then app smash by embedding their 3D models and explanatory videos into a digital Book Creator journal, allowing for immersive storytelling about how each resource shaped the nation's growth.

Launch a collaborative migration map: students research and post digital 'story cards' on a Padlet world map, each card representing a migrant group or individual who contributed to Australia's development, including their reasons for migration and the impact on society.

Learners create a Gold Rush podcast series, narrating stories from the perspectives of migrants, Indigenous Australians, and colonial settlers. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and dramatic storytelling to explore how resource discovery shaped nations and changed lives.

Students explore the Minecraft: The Hall of Inventions world, then select a historical Australian invention or resource. They research its significance and build a new exhibit in Minecraft, presenting how this resource or invention contributed to national growth.

Students take on the role of a historical figure (e.g., a gold miner, First Nations Australian, or migrant) and record a series of reflective video journal entries in Seesaw, documenting their experiences and perspectives during key events like the Gold Rush or migration waves.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a 'Resource Extraction Machine' with Lego Spike Prime, simulating how technology changed resource discovery and extraction in Australia. They iterate designs to maximize efficiency and minimize environmental impact, documenting their process in a digital engineering journal.

plannerText: This unit explores how the discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations, focusing on Australia's Gold Rush, migration, and the effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Students investigate historical events, analyze cause and effect, research case studies, and create timelines, infographics, and perspective-based narratives to deepen their understanding of economic, social, and environmental change.

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.t: Merge Cubes + iPads (Augmented Reality Self-Portrait Gallery)
s.d: Students create digital self-portraits using iPads, then use Merge Cubes to transform their artwork into interactive 3D AR experiences. Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash (Digital Wellbeing Journals + ChatterPix Kids)
s.d: Students maintain a digital wellbeing journal in Seesaw, reflecting on strategies for managing change and emotional responses. For key moments, they use ChatterPix Kids to animate a self-portrait or symbol, recording their voice to share personal insights or advice about navigating change, then embed these videos in their Seesaw journal.

Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). These can be shared digitally or printed for classroom discussion.

Students contribute to a shared Padlet wall, posting digital sticky notes with strategies, resources, or stories about managing change. They can comment on and 'like' each other's posts, creating a dynamic, student-driven resource bank that grows throughout the unit.

Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.

s.t: Lego Spike Prime (Design Cycle Project: 'Change Navigator' Robot)
s.d: Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to prototype a 'Change Navigator' robot with Lego Spike Prime. The robot could be programmed to deliver supportive messages, suggest strategies, or even respond to different scenarios about change (e.g., using sensors to trigger advice for feeling nervous or excited). Teams document their process and present their robots, reflecting on how technology can support wellbeing and transitions.

plannerText: This unit explores how managing change requires acceptance, knowledge, and strategies, with a focus on relationships, digital behaviours, and personal growth. Students engage in reflective journaling, digital art, empathy mapping, and collaborative discussions, culminating in creative projects that support wellbeing and positive transitions.

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages

s.d: Students design and build immersive 3D virtual 'advertising worlds' where visitors interact with persuasive media, ethical dilemmas, and alternative consumer models. Each group creates a virtual experience that challenges peers to think critically about advertising influence and sustainability, directly linking to the unit's focus on media engagement and audience response.

App smash: Students use Padlet to collaboratively brainstorm and map out the influences on their chosen product, then design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to gather real data on peer influences and advertising effectiveness. This workflow mirrors the unit's research and analysis tasks, making student inquiry visible and actionable.

Students compose and produce original persuasive jingles, slam poems, or rap songs that critique or promote ethical consumer choices. These audio creations are shared as part of their multimodal presentations, directly supporting the unit's emphasis on media, persuasion, and creative response.

Students create dynamic, data-driven infographics and digital posters that visually communicate the impact of advertising strategies, peer influence, and environmental consequences of consumer choices. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to remix product images, replacing the banned green screen kits.

Students become school reporters in Minecraft, investigating media sources, evaluating bias, and uncovering the truth behind advertising claims. This lesson directly supports critical thinking about media engagement and audience response, as outlined in the unit's central idea.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students prototype and build interactive 'ethical consumerism' arcade games using Makey Makey and everyday materials. Players must make choices about products, advertising, and sustainability, receiving real-time feedback on the consequences of their decisions. This hands-on project brings the unit's inquiry into media influence and responsible action to life through physical design and iteration.

plannerText: Students collaboratively investigate how media and advertising influence consumer choices, conduct surveys and interviews to analyse peer and social influences, and research the ethical and environmental impacts of products. They present findings through multimodal texts (including songs, advertisements, and slam poems) and take action by creating persuasive media or advocating for more ethical consumer practices.

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students design and build immersive 3D virtual 'ethical consumerism expos' where each group creates interactive booths showcasing their chosen product's advertising, influence, and environmental impact. Visitors can explore, interact, and leave digital feedback, simulating a real-world expo experience that highlights the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities.

Groups create and distribute digital surveys to the school community to gather authentic data on peer influences, advertising effectiveness, and ethical considerations in consumer choices. Students then analyse the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings as part of their inquiry into how organisations shape consumer behaviour.

Students collaboratively produce persuasive multimodal advertisements or slam poems for alternative, sustainable products using Canva's video and design tools. They leverage the 'Remove Background' feature for dynamic visuals and integrate real data to manipulate audience perception, directly linking to the unit's focus on advertising and influence.

Classes use Padlet to crowdsource and visually map the production, marketing, and disposal journeys of selected products. Each group posts images, research, and ethical/environmental findings, allowing for a collaborative, interactive exploration of the structure and function of organisations within global systems.

Students compose and record original audio advertisements, raps, or slam poems critiquing consumerism and proposing sustainable alternatives. They incorporate sound effects and persuasive language, then share their creations with the school community to spark discussion about responsible action and interconnected systems.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and program a 'sustainable supply chain robot' with Lego Spike Prime. The robot simulates moving products through different stages (production, marketing, disposal), and students iterate their designs to minimise environmental impact, reflecting on how human-made systems can be reimagined for a fairer future.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 6 students in an inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities, focusing on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices. Students collaboratively research, analyse, and present findings on selected products, create persuasive multimodal texts, and explore alternative models of consumerism that honour fairness and sustainability.

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use Delightex to collaboratively design and simulate 3D models of scientific experiments related to energy transfer, chemical changes, or planetary movement. They can virtually test hypotheses, manipulate variables, and visualize outcomes, then export their 3D worlds to share findings with peers, making the scientific method immersive and interactive.

Students create augmented reality models of electrical circuits, reversible/irreversible changes, or planetary systems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record short video explanations overlaying their AR models, demonstrating their understanding of scientific concepts and inquiry methods.

Groups design visually compelling infographics or animated presentations in Canva to communicate the results of their science fair investigations, focusing on how experimentation confirms, contests, or creates new theories. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for images of their experiments and embed data visualizations.

Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on classmates' hypotheses, experiment results, or scientific misconceptions. They analyze the data in Excel, drawing evidence-based conclusions and reflecting on the importance of data in scientific inquiry.

Students engage with the 'Minecraft: The Scientific Method' lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/the-scientific-method), where they plan and conduct experiments in a virtual world, practicing variables, fair testing, and data collection, directly linking to the unit's focus on how scientists experiment to build knowledge.

For the Design Cycle STEM project, students use Lego Spike Prime to design, build, and iterate a working model that demonstrates energy transfer (e.g., a solar-powered vehicle or a Rube Goldberg machine). They follow the full design process: identifying a scientific question, planning, prototyping, testing, and refining their model, then presenting their findings and reflecting on how their experimentation mirrors real scientific inquiry.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in scientific inquiry through hands-on experiments in physical science, chemistry, and earth/space science. Students follow the scientific method to investigate energy transfer, chemical changes, and planetary movement, culminating in a science fair where they present their own experiments and reflect on how scientists confirm, contest, and create new theories.

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

s.d: Students collaboratively design immersive 3D virtual worlds in Delightex to visualize the entire lifecycle of a consumer product, from resource extraction to disposal. They then embed Padlet boards within their worlds for peers to leave critical feedback, questions, and alternative sustainable ideas, creating a dynamic, interactive exhibition that brings the complexities of consumer choices and sustainability to life.

Students create and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to gather real data from their peers and families about consumer habits, influences, and attitudes toward sustainability. They analyze the results to identify trends and present their findings as evidence in their group investigations.

Groups use Canva to produce persuasive, data-driven digital campaigns or infographics that advocate for more ethical and sustainable consumer choices, incorporating statistics from their research and creative visual storytelling to influence their audience.

Students compose and record original slam poems, raps, or jingles in GarageBand, using literary devices and sound design to creatively persuade listeners to reflect on their consumer habits and consider alternative, sustainable models.

Students explore the concept of recycling and sustainability in the 'Radical Recycling' Minecraft Education lesson, gaining hands-on experience with recycling systems and then reflecting on how these models could be adapted or improved in their own communities.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype and 3D print innovative, sustainable packaging solutions for a commonly used product. They research the environmental impact of current packaging, ideate alternatives, design and print their prototypes, and then test and present their solutions, justifying their choices with evidence from their inquiry.

plannerText: Year 6 students investigate how influences shape consumer choices and the resulting impact on the world's sustainable future. They analyze advertising, peer influence, and environmental consequences through research, surveys, and case studies, culminating in collaborative presentations and creative action projects that advocate for just and sustainable consumer models.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human needs drive the development of nations.
lo: How human needs drive national development; Role of migration in nation building; Impacts of development on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students use Delightex to collaboratively design an immersive 3D migration journey, mapping the push and pull factors that influenced migration to Australia. They can embed audio narratives, historical artefacts, and interactive decision points to explore how human needs have shaped the development of nations.

Create a digital timeline in Canva that visually compares pre- and post-colonised Australia, layering key events, migration waves, and changes in citizenship rights. Students can app smash by incorporating iPad camera photos from their Melbourne Museum excursion and use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature for artefact images.

Facilitate a collaborative Padlet where students post and respond to migration stories, personal family histories, and reflections on citizenship. They can link to research, videos, and maps, fostering a living digital archive that connects classroom learning to real-world experiences.

Students author a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, weaving together first-person migration narratives, maps, and primary source analysis. They can include voice recordings, scanned artefacts, and interactive quizzes about the influence of systems on citizens.

Design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to the school community, gathering data on migration backgrounds, reasons for moving, and perceptions of Australian identity. Students analyse the results in Excel, then present their findings on how needs and systems influence migration and citizenship.

Using the Design Cycle, students prototype a 'Migration Machine' with Lego Spike Prime that models the journey of migrants across different terrains and obstacles. They program sensors to represent challenges (e.g., borders, resources) and iterate their designs to reflect how human needs and systems drive movement and adaptation.

plannerText: This unit explores how human needs drive the development of nations by comparing pre- and post-colonised Australia, examining the relationship between migration and needs, and analysing the influence of systems on citizens. Students engage in historical inquiry, personal narrative, museum excursions, and creative projects to understand migration, citizenship, and the shaping of Australian identity.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students design an immersive 3D 'Wellbeing Brain Museum' using Delightex, where each student curates an interactive exhibit on a different brain function, mindset strategy, or wellbeing technique. Visitors can virtually explore, interact with artifacts, and even leave digital 'gratitude notes' for each other, fostering a sense of collective identity and belonging.

Students document their personal wellbeing strategies by creating a daily 'Wellbeing Vlog' using the iPad Camera, then upload and reflect on their progress in Seesaw. This app-smashing workflow encourages self-management, goal setting, and peer feedback on strategies for health, safety, and emotional regulation.

Launch a collaborative 'Mindset Wall' on Padlet where students anonymously post real-life scenarios of fixed vs. growth mindset, then comment with supportive advice or strategies. This builds a safe, reflective digital community and helps students see diverse perspectives on learning and resilience.

Students create a digital 'Neurodiversity Storybook' in Book Creator, illustrating how different brains learn and thrive. Each page can feature a unique learning profile, myth-busting facts, and student-generated wellbeing tips, integrating research, creativity, and empathy.

Using Canva, students design vibrant infographics or posters that visually map the parts of the brain, their functions, and links to wellbeing strategies. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to personalize their posters with their own photos, making neuroscience relevant to their own identities.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students prototype a 'Wellbeing Alert System' using Makey Makey and everyday conductive materials. They design and test a physical device that triggers a sound or visual cue when a user needs a brain break, hydration, or mindfulness moment?integrating anatomy, self-management, and creative problem-solving.

plannerText: This unit explores identities as individuals and collectives through physical, emotional, social, and spiritual health and wellbeing, with a focus on the physiology and function of the brain, the impact of mindset and experiences on learning, and strategies to support health, safety, and wellbeing. Students engage in reflective, creative, and collaborative activities to deepen their understanding of neurodiversity, self-regulation, and personal growth.

ci: People recognise important events through celebrations and traditions.
lo: The traditions of our families and communities; How and why people celebrate; The similarities and differences between celebrations

s.d: Create an immersive, interactive celebration 'museum' using Delightex, where students use the iPad camera to photograph their own family celebration artefacts, costumes, or foods, then place these images into a virtual 3D gallery. Students can record short audio explanations (with teacher support) describing what each celebration means to them, allowing peers to 'walk through' and listen to each other's stories in a playful, visual environment.

Students take photos of celebration symbols or artefacts (e.g., a menorah, dragon puppet, or birthday cake) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, recording a short message about the celebration or tradition in their own words. This brings cultural objects to life and encourages oral language development.

Combine Seesaw with classroom activities by having students document their participation in cultural dances, food making, or art (like circle art) through photos and voice recordings. Families can respond with their own celebration stories, building a digital community celebration wall.

Guide students to collaboratively build a digital class book about 'Celebrations Around the World,' with each child contributing a page featuring their artwork, a photo, and a dictated or recorded story about a celebration they know. The final book can be shared with families and the wider school community.

Students use the iPad camera to take photos of themselves in celebration costumes, then use Canva's 'Remove Background' tool to place themselves into digital scenes of different celebrations (e.g., a lantern festival, Diwali, or a family picnic). These can be printed or displayed as a digital gallery.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design and create a large floor map representing a celebration (such as a festival parade route or a family gathering layout). Using the Design Cycle, they plan, build, and test Bee-Bot journeys to visit key celebration stations (e.g., food, music, dance, gift-giving), integrating sequencing, spatial reasoning, and storytelling about each celebration stop.

plannerText: This Prep HWEO unit explores how people recognise important events through celebrations and traditions, with students engaging in cultural dances, storytelling, art (such as circle art), music, and hands-on activities like making traditional foods. The unit emphasises sharing personal and family traditions, comparing global celebrations, and expressing understanding through creative, multimodal experiences.

ci: We all have roles and responsibilities in the community
lo: Roles people play; Responsibilities in the community; How working together helps everyone

s.d: Create an interactive 'Community Roles Adventure' using Delightex, where students visually map out different roles and responsibilities in their school and local community. Students then use the iPad camera to take photos of staff and helpers, uploading their own voice explanations to Seesaw, turning the school into a living, digital storybook.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom helpers, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages about their responsibilities, creating a playful digital 'talking roles' gallery.

Collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator where each student adds a page about their own classroom job or a community helper, using drawings, photos, and voice recordings to make it accessible and engaging for all learners.

Students design short role-play skits in Puppet Pals, acting out scenarios where they demonstrate responsibility, teamwork, or helping others in the community, supporting social-emotional learning through creative storytelling.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to visually sort and group images of different community roles (e.g., teacher, nurse, firefighter, gardener), then present their collages to the class, explaining the importance of each role using simple voice recordings.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design a 'Community Helpers Map' on a large mat, then plan, test, and iterate Bee-Bot routes to visit different community roles (e.g., library, office, playground). They reflect on how each stop represents a responsibility, documenting their process with photos and voice notes on Seesaw.

plannerText: In this unit, Prep students explore the central idea that everyone has roles and responsibilities in the community. Through stories, role play, visual timetables, and hands-on activities, they learn about different helpers in the school and wider community, develop essential agreements, and reflect on their own responsibilities as learners and friends.

ci: An inquiry into the natural world and its laws; the interaction between the natural world and human societies.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Human actions change the environment.
lo: How people interact with different environments; The effect people have on the environment; Ways in which people can take action and care for the environment

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Virtual Ocean Rescue' experience by combining Delightex's 3D scenes with student-created photos of local environments using the iPad camera. Students can visually document pollution or positive actions in their own surroundings, then place these images into a virtual world where they design solutions to environmental problems, directly linking to their learning about human impact on the planet.

Students take photos of their ceramic or drawn marine animals and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving each creature a voice to explain how human actions affect their environment and what kids can do to help. This makes environmental advocacy personal and memorable.

Have students document their 'action for the environment' (e.g., picking up rubbish, using reusable containers) by taking photos or short videos in Seesaw. They can then record voice-overs explaining their choices, creating a digital portfolio of sustainable actions to share with families.

Guide students to collaboratively create a digital class book about different environments and how to care for them. Each student contributes a page with a drawing, a photo, and a short audio recording describing a positive action for the planet, reinforcing the central idea that human actions change the environment.

Students create vibrant visual collages comparing healthy and unhealthy environments (e.g., clean reef vs. polluted reef) using PicCollage on iPads. They can use stickers, their own photos, and drawings to visually demonstrate cause and effect, then present their collages to the class.

Engage students in the Design Cycle by having them design and build a 'Recycling Rescue Maze' for Bee-Bots. Students prototype a maze representing an environment (e.g., ocean, park) and program Bee-Bots to navigate and 'collect' rubbish, reinforcing problem-solving and the impact of human actions on the environment.

plannerText: In this Prep 'Sharing the Planet' unit, students explore different environments (desert, marine, playground), investigate the impact of human actions such as pollution and recycling, and learn about responsibility and change. Activities include hands-on art, music inspired by the environment, excursions, and student-led action projects to care for the planet.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An inquiry into orientation in place and time; personal histories; the relationship between and the interconnectedness of individuals and civilisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: To be healthy, people???s basic needs must be met.
lo: How our brain is growing and changing; How our emotions, behaviours and actions promote relationships; How we care for our bodies

s.d: Students create an interactive 'Healthy Choices Adventure' by taking photos of their own healthy meals, exercise routines, and calming strategies using the iPad Camera, then upload and sequence them in Delightex to build a branching story where classmates make choices and see outcomes for health, energy, and mood. This empowers agency and visual storytelling while reinforcing the unit's focus on healthy habits.

Students draw or photograph their favourite healthy foods, exercise activities, or sleep routines, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices explaining why these choices help keep their bodies and minds healthy, directly connecting to the central idea of meeting basic needs.

Learners document their daily healthy habits by uploading photos or short videos of their lunchboxes, exercise, and calming techniques to Seesaw, adding voice reflections about how these habits help them feel energised and happy, supporting formative assessment and home-school connections.

Each student creates a digital 'Healthy Me' book, combining photos, drawings, and audio recordings to showcase their understanding of nutrition, exercise, sleep, and wellbeing, culminating in a class library of diverse healthy lifestyle stories that can be shared with families.

Students collaboratively design vibrant, visual posters in Canva illustrating the '7 Elements of a Healthy Lifestyle' (food, sleep, play, mind health, active learning, exercise, safety), using icons, photos, and minimal text, then display these around the classroom and digitally share with the school community.

Using the Design Cycle, students design and build a 'Healthy Habits Obstacle Course' on a large floor mat, coding Bee-Bots to travel to stations representing healthy food, exercise, sleep, and mindfulness. At each stop, students explain the importance of the habit, integrating robotics, spatial reasoning, and health education in a hands-on STEM project.

plannerText: This Prep unit explores the central idea that to be healthy, people's basic needs must be met, focusing on nutrition, exercise, sleep, wellbeing, and self-regulation. Students engage in activities such as classifying foods, learning about the 'Zones of Regulation', creating healthy lifestyle stories, and reflecting on their own habits through visual, hands-on, and collaborative experiences.

ci: People express their emotions through the way they communicate.
lo: Different ways to communicate emotions; How art, music and movement express feelings; Understanding others' expressions through body language and facial cues

s.d: Students create interactive digital storyboards in Delightex, then bring their own facial expressions and body language to life by recording themselves as characters in Puppet Pals. This workflow allows students to visually and physically express a range of emotions, then sequence and narrate their stories, directly connecting to the unit's focus on communicating feelings beyond words.

Students take photos of their own expressive faces or artwork, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record their voices describing different emotions. This empowers students to explore how tone, pitch, and expression combine to communicate feelings.

Students document and reflect on their emotional expressions through photos, drawings, and short audio clips, creating a digital portfolio of how they communicate feelings in different contexts (art, sport, drama, playground). Teachers can prompt students to respond to scenarios using expressive selfies or voice recordings.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'Feelings Book' where each page features a different emotion, illustrated with their own artwork or photos, and accompanied by audio recordings of students explaining or acting out the emotion. This supports multimodal communication and peer learning.

Students design vibrant digital posters or mood boards using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' feature to collage their expressive faces, body poses, and symbolic images. These posters visually represent how emotions can be communicated through color, pattern, and imagery, linking to the unit's art and sport connections.

Using the Design Cycle, students design and decorate Bee-Bot mats with different emotional scenarios (e.g., playground, sports field, art gallery). They then program Bee-Bots to navigate to various emotions, explaining their choices and acting out the corresponding feelings at each stop. This hands-on STEM project integrates coding, design, and emotional literacy in a playful, physical way.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring how people express emotions through communication, body language, facial expressions, art, music, and sport. Activities include studying artists like Frida Kahlo, creating expressive portraits, designing team identities, and using drama and movement to convey feelings, with a strong focus on multimodal and nonverbal communication.

ci: Systems are involved in preparing products for consumers.
lo: Where products come from; The systems products go through; How people choose their products

s.d: Students collaboratively design an interactive digital flowchart in Delightex to visually map the journey of a product (e.g., milk or apples) from farm to table, then use Seesaw to record voiceovers explaining each stage, creating a multimedia 'choose your own adventure' style experience for peers to explore.

Learners create a digital storybook documenting the transformation of a raw product into a consumer good, using photos, drawings, and audio narration to sequence each step, making the process accessible and engaging for early readers.

Students take photos with iPads of different products and their packaging, then use PicCollage to visually sort and label the stages of processing, transportation, and storage, supporting hands-on exploration of systems.

Classes watch age-appropriate videos from Clickview's 'Farm to Table' or 'War on Waste' series, then discuss and document the systems involved in preparing products for consumers, reinforcing real-world connections.

Children animate photos of products (like apples or milk cartons) using ChatterPix Kids, giving each product a 'voice' to describe its journey through the system, supporting oral language and sequencing skills.

Using the Design Cycle, students design and build a large floor map representing the stages a product travels (e.g., farm, factory, shop, home). They program Bee-Bots to follow the correct sequence, iterating their map and code to ensure accuracy, and reflecting on how systems work together to deliver products to consumers.

plannerText: This unit explores the systems involved in preparing products for consumers, focusing on the journey from raw materials to finished goods. Students investigate and sequence the stages of production, processing, transportation, and waste, using hands-on activities, digital storytelling, and collaborative inquiry to understand how everyday items reach them.

ci: Weather affects our lives in different ways.
lo: Types of weather conditions; How weather impacts our lives (clothing, activities, food, feelings); Why weather changes (seasons, patterns)

s.d: Students become 'weather reporters' by capturing daily weather changes using the iPad Camera, then app smash into Delightex to create interactive, animated weather scenes. They can drag and drop digital clothing, foods, and activities onto avatars to show how weather impacts daily life, then record their voices explaining their choices.

Students take photos of their own weather-related drawings or classroom weather charts, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with talking mouths. Each student records a short weather report or explains how the weather affects what they wear or do that day, building confidence and oral language skills.

Students collaboratively build a digital class book documenting different types of weather, clothing, and activities for each season. Each page features student-drawn illustrations, voice recordings, and photos, making the book highly visual and accessible for Year 1 learners.

Students use Seesaw to document their daily weather observations by uploading photos, sorting digital clothing and activity cards, and recording their reflections. Teachers can set up interactive activities where students match weather types to appropriate clothing or activities, supporting formative assessment.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJr showing how weather changes affect their day. For example, they can program a character to choose an umbrella when it rains or put on a hat when it is sunny, reinforcing cause and effect through coding and storytelling.

Using the Design Cycle, students design and build a large floor map with different weather zones (sunny, rainy, snowy, windy). They program Bee-Bots to travel to the correct zone based on a weather scenario card, then physically dress up avatars or dolls in weather-appropriate clothing at each stop, combining robotics, problem-solving, and hands-on making.

plannerText: The unit engages Year 1 students in exploring how weather affects daily life, including clothing, activities, and food choices. Students investigate weather patterns, collect and graph data, compare seasonal changes, and express their understanding through creative activities such as weather reports, tableaux, and music. The unit emphasizes student agency, hands-on inquiry, and visual documentation of learning.

ci: Living things have structures that allow them to survive in different environments.
lo: The physical structure of plants and bees; How features support survival in different environments; Human impact on living things

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Plant & Bee Adaptation AR Gallery' where students use the iPad Camera to photograph their own bean plants and bee sculptures, then upload these into Delightex to create interactive, explorable 3D dioramas showing how living things adapt to different environments. Students can add voice notes explaining each adaptation, making their learning visible and shareable in a truly innovative way.

Students create talking plants or bees by drawing or photographing their chosen living thing, then using ChatterPix Kids to animate it with a voiceover explaining how its structures help it survive. This brings scientific concepts to life in a playful, memorable format.

Students document the growth and adaptation of their broad bean plants in a digital diary using Book Creator. They can add photos, drawings, audio reflections, and simple labels to visually track changes and explain how environmental factors affect plant survival.

Combine Seesaw with the iPad Camera and drawing tools to create a 'Plant and Animal Adaptation Detective' activity. Students photograph different plants and animals around the school, annotate the images with arrows and labels, and record voice explanations about the adaptations they observe.

Students make vibrant visual collages comparing the structures of different living things (e.g., leaves, stems, animal features) and how these help them survive in their environments. They can use stickers, cut-outs, and their own photos to create side-by-side adaptation comparisons.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design and build a 'Survival Maze' for Bee-Bots, representing different environments (e.g., desert, rainforest, pond). They program Bee-Bots to navigate the maze, stopping at key adaptation stations where they must explain how a plant or animal survives in that environment. This hands-on project integrates coding, design, and scientific thinking.

plannerText: Students explore the structures and adaptations of living things through hands-on activities like growing broad beans in different conditions, scientific drawing, and clay bee models. They investigate plant and animal adaptations, document growth, and reflect on how environmental factors influence survival, with strong emphasis on visual learning, student agency, and creative expression.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.
lo: How technology has changed over time; The purpose and significance of artefacts; How the past connects to the present and future

s.d: Launch a collaborative virtual 'Museum of Time' where students upload photos of family artefacts, record short voice stories about their significance, and co-create a digital timeline that visually connects their personal histories to broader changes in technology, dance, and daily life.

Students take photos of old and new artefacts (e.g., phones, toys, dance shoes) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving each artefact a 'voice' to tell its story from the past, present, or imagined future.

Create a class digital book where each student adds a page comparing an artefact or tradition from their family's past with their life today, using photos, drawings, and audio reflections to highlight connections across generations.
Students document their learning journey by uploading photos of timelines, artefacts, and family trees, and record voice memos reflecting on how their family and technology have changed over time.

Design a vibrant, visual timeline poster in Canva, app-smashing with iPad camera photos of artefacts and student artwork, to display the evolution of technology, dance, and family traditions in the classroom.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design and build a large floor timeline map, then program Bee-Bots to travel from 'past' to 'present' to 'future' artefacts, narrating key changes at each stop. This hands-on activity reinforces sequencing, spatial reasoning, and historical understanding.

plannerText: Students explore how history and culture connect the past, present, and future by investigating artefacts, creating timelines, comparing family traditions, and predicting future changes. Activities include sorting photos, discussing technological evolution, and designing future devices, with strong emphasis on visual, hands-on, and collaborative learning.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.
lo: How to identify and manage emotions; How players' choices affect relationships; Our responsibilities when playing games

s.d: Students collaboratively design a digital board game about playground fairness using Delightex, then bring their characters to life by recording voice-acted game scenarios in Puppet Pals. This app smash allows students to explore multiple perspectives and practice resolving conflict through creative storytelling.

Students document and reflect on their own game creations and playground experiences by capturing photos, drawing, and recording voice-overs in Seesaw. They can share strategies for fair play and problem-solving, building a digital portfolio of social learning moments.

Students take photos of their favorite playground games or team mascots, then animate them with their own voices using ChatterPix Kids to explain the rules, share tips for teamwork, or describe how to handle disagreements during play.

Students create visual collages of 'What Makes a Game Fair?' using photos from the iPad camera, stickers, and drawings. They can visually sort examples of positive and negative playground behaviors, then present their collages to the class for discussion.

Students design digital posters in Canva that illustrate the essential agreements for games and respectful play. They can use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to insert themselves into their posters, making the agreements feel personal and relevant.

Using the Design Cycle, students invent and prototype a new playground game that incorporates Bee-Bots as moving game pieces. They plan, test, and refine their game rules, then teach classmates how to play, reflecting on fairness and teamwork throughout the process.

plannerText: This unit explores how students learn about themselves through the games they play, focusing on fairness, rules, teamwork, and resolving conflict. Activities include creating essential agreements, reflecting on emotions and behavior during play, building team identities, and using digital tools to document and share learning about positive social interactions.

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

s.d: Students create an interactive, virtual 'museum' of family artefacts and traditions using Delightex. They photograph their own artefacts or celebration items with the iPad Camera, then app smash into Delightex to design immersive, clickable exhibits that include audio explanations and digital drawings, allowing classmates to 'visit' and learn about each other's cultures in a playful, game-like environment.

Students photograph a personal or cultural artefact, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the artefact so it 'talks' and shares its story, beliefs, or cultural significance in the student's own voice. This brings artefacts to life and builds oral language skills while celebrating cultural diversity.

Each student creates a digital book documenting their family's traditions, celebrations, and artefacts. They combine photos, voice recordings, and drawings to express how these elements reflect their beliefs and values, then share their books with peers for a virtual cultural showcase.

Students design vibrant digital posters in Canva about a chosen cultural celebration or tradition, using the 'Remove Background' feature to layer themselves into scenes. They then upload and narrate their posters in Seesaw, explaining the meaning behind the celebration and its artefacts.

Students create short animated skits in Puppet Pals, using characters and backgrounds inspired by their own or classmates' cultural stories and rituals. They script and record voiceovers to dramatize how artefacts are used in celebrations, fostering empathy and understanding of diverse perspectives.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students design and build a large floor map representing a multicultural festival, with stations for different cultural artefacts and traditions. Using the Design Cycle, they plan, test, and refine Bee-Bot routes to 'visit' each station, programming the Bee-Bots to stop and play a short audio fact or sound related to each artefact, integrating robotics, mapping, and cultural inquiry.

plannerText: This unit explores how artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures. Students investigate their own and others' traditions, celebrations, and cultural expressions through artefacts, music, dance, and storytelling, culminating in creative projects that celebrate diversity and foster intercultural understanding.

ci: Technology can influence the way ecosystems can survive.
lo: An ecosystem has animals, plants and microorganisms; Technology can cause ecosystems to adapt and evolve; Technology can help or hinder ecosystems

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of different ecosystems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app on iPads. They record short ChatterPix Kids voiceovers as 'ecosystem reporters' explaining how technology (like satellites or robots) is helping or harming their chosen ecosystem, then overlay these on their Merge Cube models for an immersive, hands-on AR experience.

Students document the evolution of technology in ecosystems by capturing photos or videos of their class 'robot habitats' and narrating their observations. They can use Seesaw's drawing and annotation tools to highlight how technology changes the balance or function of their mini-ecosystems.

Students code simple stories showing how a robot or piece of technology interacts with an ecosystem. For example, they might animate a bee-bot pollinating flowers or a robot cleaning up pollution, helping them visualise cause and effect in a playful, accessible way.

Students collaboratively build a digital book documenting their learning journey about technology and ecosystems. Each page can feature photos, voice recordings, and drawings showing before-and-after scenarios of ecosystems influenced by technology.

s.t: Minecraft: Its Good To Be Green
s.d: Students explore a sustainable building in Minecraft Education, learning how technology can support ecosystem health. They reflect on how design choices impact the environment, connecting directly to the unit's focus on technology's influence on survival. (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/its-good-to-be-green)

Using the Design Cycle, students design and build a model ecosystem (e.g., a garden or forest) and program Bee-Bots to act as 'helpers'?such as pollinators or waste collectors. They iterate on their designs to see how different Bee-Bot actions can support or disrupt the balance of their ecosystem, fostering hands-on STEM learning.

plannerText: This unit explores how technology can influence the survival and adaptation of ecosystems. Students investigate the roles of robotics and digital tools in society, engage in coding and persuasive writing about robots, and reflect on ethical and functional impacts of technology on natural systems through hands-on and inquiry-based activities.

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources

s.d: Students create talking energy source characters using ChatterPix Kids, giving each energy type (solar, fossil fuel, wind, etc.) a voice to explain how it works and its impact on the planet. These animated characters are then uploaded to Seesaw for a class-wide interactive energy debate, allowing students to respond to each other's posts with their own energy character's perspective.

Students design and code simple animations showing the journey of energy from source to use (e.g., sun to solar panel to light bulb), visually demonstrating energy transformation and sustainability concepts in a hands-on, creative way.
